app.name: Agent Atelier
app.tagline: Build, test and understand agents from the inside.
language.label: Language
run.title: Research Brief Agent
run.question.label: Research question
run.question.help: This is the small goal given to the agent. In this chapter it searches only the built-in local evidence cards.
run.question.placeholder: How do agent budgets improve safety?
budget.title: Run budget
budget.help: A hard limit enforced by Python. Set tool calls to 0 to see the engine block the search even when the provider requests it.
tools.title: Allowed tools
timeline.title: Public event timeline
timeline.help: A safe record of what the application did: proposed action, validated tool, result and stop. It is not private chain of thought.
timeline.empty: Run the agent to see validated actions and results.
evidence.title: Evidence
metrics.title: Metrics
guide.name: Paula
guide.ready: Let's inspect every safe decision the agent makes.
guide.running: The provider proposes actions; the engine validates them.
guide.completed: The brief cites only evidence returned by an allowed tool.
guide.insufficient: There is not enough local evidence, so the agent stops honestly.
guide.milestone: Milestone built! Indy noticed too. Now prove that this piece behaves correctly.
indy.label: Indy, Siamese cat mascot
indy.resting_label: Indy sleeping quietly in the sidebar
indy.help.open: Ask Indy for an optional hint
indy.help.title: A small emergency hint
celebration.title: You built the complete Agent Atelier!
celebration.body: You did more than click through a tutorial: you built, tested and explained every layer of an agent harness.
celebration.message: Paula and Indy award you the Agent Atelier Builder trophy.
celebration.follow: Would you like to follow the creator or explore more projects?
audio.original: Original cozy-jazz ambience — no downloaded song.
audio.blocked: Playback was blocked — use the player shown below.
chapter.label: Chapter
chapter.path.title: Learning path
chapter.path.help: Choose an available chapter. Your progress is saved in this browser.
progress.saved: Progress is saved in this browser.
progress.reset_body: This removes completed chapters and returns to Chapter 0. Language and audio volume are kept.
completion.missing_build: To finish: complete the three code steps and pass the code checkpoint.
completion.missing_test: To finish: make a prediction and obtain the chapter's required lab evidence.
completion.missing_reflection: To finish: explain what rule the application controls and what evidence proved it.
completion.ready: Everything required is complete. You can finish this chapter now.
completion.done: Chapter finished. You can continue to the next chapter or review this one.
completion.guided: Not finished yet. Paula has taken you to the next required step.
chapter.0.title: Agent, workflow or chatbot?
chapter.0.summary: Learn who chooses the next action and where application control lives.
chapter.1.title: The smallest bounded loop
chapter.1.summary: Follow a finite loop with explicit budgets and terminal states.
chapter.2.title: Typed tools and schemas
chapter.2.summary: Validate names, fields and types before any tool executes.
chapter.3.title: State, context and memory
chapter.3.summary: See what belongs to one run, what is selected for a decision and what persists.
chapter.4.title: Planning and progress
chapter.4.summary: Turn a goal into checkable steps without confusing a plan with a guarantee.
chapter.5.title: Events, traces and debugging
chapter.5.summary: Expose safe execution facts while redacting secrets and private reasoning.
chapter.6.title: Guardrails and untrusted data
chapter.6.summary: Keep retrieved instructions inside the data boundary and enforce input limits.
chapter.7.title: Reproducible evaluation
chapter.7.summary: Run concrete scenarios with exact expected outcomes instead of vague quality claims.
chapter.8.title: Human approval
chapter.8.summary: Pause sensitive actions and grant a scoped, single-use permission.
chapter.9.title: Cost, latency and budgets
chapter.9.summary: Measure abstract usage and see cost and latency limits fail independently.
chapter.10.title: Complete Research Brief application
chapter.10.summary: Run the integrated system and inspect each layer only when you need it.
chapter.reading.title: Chapter overview
flow.guide.overview: Start here. Read the three promises below: what you will learn, which harness piece you will add and what evidence must prove it works.
flow.guide.build: Now build it. Follow the three numbered code steps from top to bottom, run each simulated command and complete the missing identifier.
flow.guide.test: Good. Now make the concept fail and succeed in the interactive lab. Each result is evidence, not decoration.
flow.guide.reflect: Pause and explain the result: the provider proposes an action, but your application owns validation, permission and stopping. Check that every required proof is green.
flow.guide.complete: You have built and proved this layer. Mark the chapter complete; the studio and the harness will gain their next piece.
flow.guide.detail.overview: Hi! In this chapter we are working on {title}. The goal is: {outcome} We will take it slowly: first understand the boundary, then build it, test both success and failure, and finally explain what the evidence proved.
flow.guide.detail.build: Great, let's build the real harness piece together. You are on step {step} of {total}, in {file}. Why this step exists: {purpose}. Now read the small snippet, run its simulated check and use Next step when the idea is clear.
flow.guide.detail.test: Nice work — the code boundary is built. Now we must prove it behaves as promised: {proof} First record what you expect, then run every contrasting case. A result only counts when you can explain why the application produced it.
flow.guide.detail.reflect: Almost there. Tell me the story of the experiment in your own words: name the rule owned by the application, mention one result you actually observed and connect the two. You do not need formal language; clarity matters more.
flow.guide.detail.complete: You built, tested and explained {title}. Check the evidence once more, record the milestone and look at the studio: this new harness piece is now part of the room and the final agent.
flow.check.overview.1: Read what you will be able to explain at the end.
flow.check.overview.2: Find the exact file or boundary added to the harness.
flow.check.overview.3: Know which visible result will prove the concept.
flow.check.build.2: Read why this code exists, not only what it says.
flow.check.build.3: Run the check; on the final step, complete the `____` identifier.
flow.check.test.1: Write a prediction before unlocking the controls.
flow.check.test.2: Run all contrasting cases ({observed} of {required} observed).
flow.check.test.3: Compare the outputs and identify the application rule that caused them.
flow.check.reflect.1: Name the rule controlled by the application.
flow.check.reflect.2: Mention one concrete result from the lab.
flow.check.reflect.3: Connect rule and evidence in your own words.
flow.check.complete.1: Confirm the construction and behaviour evidence are complete.
flow.check.complete.3: Continue when you are ready; the next layer builds on this one.
flow.action.finish_build: Complete the code challenge to continue
flow.action.finish_test: Collect every lab result to continue
flow.action.return_build: Return to the missing construction step
flow.action.return_test: Return to the missing lab evidence
lab.method: Before clicking, predict the result. Then run every case, compare the outputs and explain which rule in the harness caused the difference.
prediction.prompt: Before you run the lab: what do you expect to happen?
prediction.help: Write one concrete prediction and a reason. The controls unlock after you commit it, so you can compare expectation with evidence.
prediction.placeholder: I expect... because the application rule...
prediction.submit: Commit my prediction and unlock the lab
prediction.too_short: Be more specific: predict one visible result and explain why.
prediction.saved: Prediction saved. Now run the cases and compare the evidence.
lab.10.unsupported_question: What is the population of Mars in 2025?
reflection.prompt: Explain in your own words: what does the application control here, and which result proved it?
reflection.help: Use the chapter outcome and the observed lab results. A useful explanation names both the rule and its evidence.
reflection.placeholder: The application controls... I know because...
reflection.too_short: Add a little more detail: name the application-owned rule and one result you observed.
reflection.success: Clear explanation recorded. You connected the code to observable behaviour.
workshop.title: Code workshop
workshop.format: Type only the missing identifier — one word, without quotes or punctuation.
workshop.output.ready: Ready. Run the command to inspect its expected local result.
workshop.output.passed: PASS · This command is represented by the repository's real automated tests.
workshop.incorrect: That identifier does not connect the pieces correctly. Inspect the blank again. Expected: {answer}
workshop.hint: Not yet. Find ____ in the code above, then look for the same concept or field name in the previous steps. Type only that identifier.
provider.title: Optional model provider
provider.label: Execution provider
provider.openai: OpenAI Responses API · may incur cost
provider.ready: Real provider is configured on this local server. The key is not exposed to the browser.
provider.not_ready: Real provider is unavailable. Follow docs/REAL_PROVIDER.md; the free simulator remains fully functional.
provider.safe_launcher: The real provider is locked. Close this preview and run OPEN_AGENT_ATELIER_REAL.bat to enter a key safely outside the browser. The simulator remains free.
provider.consent: I understand that selecting a real provider sends this question to an external API and may incur cost.
provider.consent_required: Confirm the external API and cost notice before running a real provider.
lab.title: Interactive lab
lab.default_question: How do agent budgets improve safety?
lab.0.prompt: Classify the system: given a support goal and changing conversation state, it selects at runtime whether to search documentation, request an order ID or escalate; the application restricts those choices.
lab.0.correct: Correct. The system chooses among allowed actions while the application keeps control of tools and escalation.
lab.0.incorrect: Not quite. Focus on who chooses the next action: the developer fixed it, or the system selects it at runtime?
lab.1.prompt: Run the same goal twice. First allow tools; then remove tool permission through a zero-call budget. Compare the terminal status instead of guessing what the model intended.
lab.1.normal_result: Status: {status}. The loop completed in {steps} steps with {tools} tool call. The provider proposed actions, but the harness controlled execution.
lab.1.blocked_result: Status: {status}. Tool calls: {tools}. Stop condition: {stop}. The harness stopped before executing the proposed search.
lab.1.ready: The controls below are the Chapter 1 lab. Compare a normal run with a zero-tool budget.
lab.2.prompt: Send a proposed tool call through the real application-owned schema.
lab.2.contract: Allowed contract: search_local_corpus requires exactly one non-empty text field named query.
lab.3.prompt: Save one preference, start another run and compare the three boxes.
lab.4.prompt: Create a plan, inspect each success condition, then simulate the application confirming one condition at a time. A real system must connect that confirmation to evidence or a tool result.
lab.4.question: Planning goal
lab.5.prompt: Compare a safe event, a payload containing a secret and a forbidden private event.
lab.5.result_safe: Published: {type}. The timeline exposes the action, not hidden reasoning.
lab.5.result_secret: Published after redaction. The public value is {value}; the secret never reaches the timeline.
lab.5.result_private: Rejected. Private provider reasoning is not an allowed public event type.
lab.6.prompt: Inspect retrieved content and see why even a normal document remains untrusted data.
lab.6.result_safe: Kept inside {boundary}. Source {source} may be read as evidence, but never as application instructions.
lab.6.result_blocked: Prompt injection detected in source {source}. It remains inside {boundary} and is not executed.
lab.7.prompt: Run the real deterministic evaluation suite and inspect every expected outcome.
scenario.successful_grounded_brief: A supported question must finish with citations returned by the tool.
scenario.insufficient_evidence: An unrelated question must stop instead of inventing an answer.
scenario.zero_tool_budget: A zero-call budget must prevent tool execution.
scenario.forbidden_tool: A provider must not execute a tool outside the allowlist.
scenario.invented_citation: A final answer must not cite evidence the tool never returned.
lab.8.prompt: Propose exporting a brief. Nothing executes until a person approves the exact action.
lab.8.status.pending: Pending: export_brief(format=markdown, brief_id=B-001) cannot execute until a person decides.
lab.8.status.approved: Approved for this exact export only. The capability is kept internal and can be consumed once.
lab.8.status.denied: Denied. No export capability was granted and the action cannot execute.
lab.8.status.consumed: Executed once. The scoped capability is now consumed and cannot be reused.
lab.9.prompt: Compare three controlled runs. Only one variable crosses its limit, so you can see that cost and latency are independent policies.
lab.10.prompt: Run the complete offline application. Start with the outcome, then expand plan, evidence, events, usage and evaluation.
lab.10.question: Research question
learning.progress: Evidence collected: {current} of {total}. Complete the guided comparison before marking the chapter.
learning.ready: Evidence complete. You can now mark this chapter as completed.
learning.mastered: Chapter completed. Re-run the lab whenever you want to review the evidence.
chapter.0.outcome: Explain the practical difference between a chatbot, a fixed workflow and a bounded agent.
chapter.0.piece: Architecture boundary: provider proposes; application authorizes and executes.
chapter.0.proof: You classify a runtime action-selection example and justify who controls the available actions.
chapter.1.proof: The same goal completes with an allowed tool and stops before execution when its tool budget is zero.
chapter.2.outcome: Reject malformed tool calls before implementation code runs.
chapter.2.piece: src/agent_atelier/tools.py: allowlist, argument contract and executor.
chapter.2.proof: One valid call executes; missing, extra and wrongly typed arguments are blocked.
chapter.3.outcome: Decide what is temporary, selected for the next decision or deliberately retained.
chapter.3.proof: A new run resets temporary state while the chosen session fact persists until cleared.
chapter.4.outcome: Represent planning as inspectable state with success conditions, not persuasive prose.
chapter.4.piece: src/agent_atelier/planning.py: ordered steps and controlled transitions.
chapter.4.proof: Only the active step can advance, and the next step activates after a simulated verified condition.
chapter.5.outcome: Debug actions and outcomes without publishing secrets or private reasoning.
chapter.5.proof: A safe event is published, a secret field is redacted and a private event type is rejected.
chapter.6.outcome: Treat retrieved instructions as untrusted data rather than new system authority.
chapter.6.piece: src/agent_atelier/guardrails.py: input limits, warnings and data wrapper.
chapter.6.proof: Both documents remain data; the injected one is additionally flagged without pretending pattern matching is sufficient defence.
chapter.7.outcome: Turn safety claims into repeatable scenarios with exact expected outcomes.
chapter.7.proof: Every scenario reports expected status, actual status and named checks, not a vague quality score.
chapter.8.outcome: Pause a sensitive action and bind approval to exact arguments for one use.
chapter.8.piece: src/agent_atelier/approvals.py: request, decision, scoped capability and consumption.
chapter.8.proof: Export cannot run while pending or denied; an approved capability works once and is never displayed.
chapter.9.outcome: Explain why cost and latency need separate measurable limits.
chapter.9.proof: Controlled cases pass both budgets, exceed only cost or exceed only latency.
chapter.10.outcome: Connect all harness layers and state the system's honest limitations.
chapter.10.piece: src/agent_atelier/final_app.py: plan, bounded run, evidence, usage and evaluation.
chapter.10.proof: A grounded question completes with known citations; an unsupported question stops with an explicit limitation.
chapter.0.pose: Paula points out the first system boundary while Indy sits nearby and observes.
chapter.1.pose: Paula reads the loop carefully while Indy watches the first execution.
chapter.2.pose: Paula compares the tool schema while Indy waits beside the workbench.
chapter.3.pose: Paula takes notes about memory lifetimes while Indy quietly washes a paw.
chapter.4.pose: Paula studies the plan step by step while Indy settles beside the planning board.
chapter.5.pose: Paula reviews the public trace while Indy keeps watch near the debugging screen.
chapter.6.pose: Paula explains the safety boundary while Indy turns toward the warning signal.
chapter.7.pose: Paula checks the evaluation results while Indy observes the passing scenarios.
chapter.8.pose: Paula presents the approval checkpoint while Indy waits for the human decision.
chapter.9.pose: Paula celebrates a budget that holds while Indy rests after the measurements.
chapter.10.pose: Paula and Indy pose with the completed atelier and its fully assembled harness.
privacy.notice: No API key, internet connection or private chain of thought is used.
prediction.help_choice: Choose the result you expect. There is no typing: commit one option, then compare it with the evidence.
prediction.select: Choose one prediction before unlocking the experiment.
reflection.prompt_choice: Choose the explanation that correctly connects the application rule with the evidence.
reflection.help_choice: Compare the three mental models. Feedback explains why each one is safe or misleading.
reflection.incorrect: That explanation gives control to the wrong layer. The safe conclusion is: {answer}
workshop.format_choice: Choose the identifier that correctly connects the code. No typing is required.
study.title: Your complete Agent Atelier study guide
study.intro: Read the whole path first or open one chapter at a time. Every card explains the idea, a concrete example, a common mistake and the code pieces you will meet.
flow.guide.detail.reflect_choice: Almost there. Compare the three explanations and choose the one that gives validation, permission and stopping to the application. The feedback will explain the misconception if you choose a tempting but unsafe model.
flow.check.test.1.choice: Choose a prediction before unlocking the controls.
flow.check.reflect.1.choice: Find the option that gives control to the application.
flow.check.reflect.2.choice: Check that the option cites a concrete lab result.
flow.check.reflect.3.choice: Select the explanation that connects the rule and its evidence.
flow.action.finish_reflect_choice: Choose the safe explanation to continue
workshop.choose_label: Which identifier correctly fills the ____ blank?
paula.details.open: Ask Paula for a deeper technical explanation
paula.details.summary_template: Core idea: {plain} Repository boundary: {piece} Observable proof: {proof} Example: {example}
prediction.saved_detail: Prediction recorded: “{prediction}” Now run the contrasting cases and compare expectation with evidence.
reflection.success_detail: Correct mental model. {why} The concrete example that anchors it is: {evidence}
reflection.incorrect_detail: This option reflects a common mistake: {mistake} The safer explanation is: {answer}
